stories.title: NYC’s Conversion Break Producing Far More Units Than New-Construction Sweetener
stories.excerpt: It’s been well documented and much lamented just how few ground-up residential units are being developed and constructed in New York City due to the limitations of 485-x, a 2-year-old state tax incentive that appears to guide most developers to a 99-unit limit on new multifamily buildings in much of the city in order for them to avoid paying construction wage minimums of $40 an hour. This becomes especially stark when compared to the robust productivity of 467-m, another 2-year-old state tax break, this one for office-to-residential conversions and without any wage requirements. Now, one enterprising industry professional has put the comparison in stark terms, and it doesn’t bode well for 485-x. According to Nate Bliss, founder and principal of Latent Urban Ventures and a former chief of staff to the deputy mayor for housing in Eric Adams’s administration, the average office-to-residential conversion project in the city — including completed and planned projects under 467-m — produces 298 apartments. By comparison, the average new construction project under 485-x creates a grand total of just 39.4 apartments per project. “The problems with the 485-x program were well known at the time it passed,” said Bliss. “The biggest surprise to me is just how much has been generated under the 467-m program. If you look at this first wave of projects, we’ve got over twice as many permanently affordable apartments delivered with 467-m as we do with 485-x.” Bliss, who utilized pipeline data — meaning that his numbers included both projects that are underway and projects in the application stage — found that there are currently 55 conversions filed or underway under 467-m, with another 15 in active plan review. In total, these projects are slated to produce up to 20,876 apartments, 5,219 of them potentially designated permanently affordable. As for new construction under 485-x, he found 301 prospective registrations for a total of 11,869 intended apartments, with 2,557 afford
bullets: NYC 467-m conversions avg 298 units; 485-x new construction avg under 100 units.
$40/hr wage requirement in 485-x deters ground-up builds; 467-m has no wage mandate.
Conversion pipeline outpacing new supply; office-to-resi supply flood threatens new-build economics.
stories.summary: Office-to-residential conversions under NYC's 467-m tax break average 298 units per project, nearly triple the typical new multifamily building size under 485-x (which caps around 99 units to avoid $40/hour prevailing wage). The 485-x wage requirement is suppressing ground-up construction, while 467-m's lack of wage mandates incentivizes conversions. Bliss's data shows conversion productivity is outpacing new-construction incentives substantially.
stories.impact: Manhattan and outer-borough multifamily developers face a structurally broken incentive stack. The 485-x wage floor makes new ground-up delivery uneconomical at lower unit counts, forcing smaller footprints and fewer total units. Conversions flood the market with rental supply at faster pace, pressuring rents and reducing development IRRs for traditional new construction. Developers should pivot to conversion plays or lobby for 485-x reform to restore new-build competitiveness in NYC.

stories.title: Office Access Platform SwiftConnect Acquires HID SAFE
stories.excerpt: SwiftConnect, which bills itself as a connected access office property platform, announced Wednesday that it has acquired HID SAFE, a leading company in the field of managing access to properties. The acquisition will enhance Stamford, Conn.-based SwiftConnect’s physical access platform with HID SAFE’s identity governance capability, said Matt Kopel, co-CEO of SwiftConnect. Kopel declined to state the financial and stock terms of the deal. The transaction builds on HID’s prior participation in SwiftConnect’s Series B funding round, Kopel said. HID SAFE will hold a minority, non-controlling interest in SwiftConnect as a result of the acquisition. SwiftConnect will remain an independent company, controlled and managed by its existing leadership team, which includes co-CEO Chip Kruger. “It starts with customer feedback,” Kopel said of the HID SAFE acquisition. “What we’ve built so far with our connected network is something that customers like. One of the things that customers ask for is a governance layer on top. We help put identities and credentials from one system to another for potential customers, from enterprises to their landlord. Customers were asking for us to expand our capabilities to include this governance to figure out where somebody’s identity should go and where they should have access based on certain attributes about them or approvals.” As an investor in SwiftConnect prior to its acquisition, HID SAFE was already a shareholder, but will now increase its minority share in the company with no operational influence, said Kopel. While the HID name will go away, the SAFE brand will remain on the platform for now. It will be re-evaluated over the coming months as to whether it will be kept longterm, Kopel added. The integration process has already begun, with both companies serving large enterprises globally. “HID SAFE has earned the trust of the world’s most security-driven organizations for more than two decades,” said Björn Lidefelt, executi
bullets: SwiftConnect acquires HID SAFE; HID SAFE retains minority non-controlling stake.
Platform integrates physical access with identity governance for office properties.
Tech consolidation in commercial real estate access/management space; minimal direct multifamily impact.
stories.summary: Office-focused tech platform SwiftConnect acquired HID SAFE, a property access management company, to add identity governance capabilities. HID SAFE retains a minority stake. The deal integrates physical access control with identity management for enterprise and commercial tenants. Financial terms undisclosed.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. This is a vertical consolidation in commercial office property tech. Indirect signal: office modernization investment may support office-to-residential conversion economics (better-managed, higher-value conversion assets), but the story itself is office-centric.

stories.title: Multifamily Loan Balances Jumped 53% Since 2019
stories.excerpt: U.S. bank real estate loan balances totaled $6.14 trillion in the first quarter of 2026, according to CRED iQ data. Since the first quarter of 2019, the composition of that real estate loan balance book has shifted measurably. Multifamily balances grew the fastest in percentage terms, rising about 53 percent, while the much larger residential book grew about 17 percent. In dollar terms the ordering differs, with core commercial real estate and residential each adding more than $440 billion while multifamily added about $229 billion. As of the first quarter of 2026, multifamily balances stood at an index of 153 (about 53 percent above the 2019 level), core CRE at 132 (roughly 32 percent), construction and development at 128 (around 28 percent), and residential at 117 (approximately 17 percent). Residential, which comprises mortgages on one-family, two-family, three-family and four-family residential properties and home equity, remained the largest category at $3.1 trillion, roughly half of all bank real estate lending, followed by core CRE at $1.92 trillion, multifamily at $665 billion, and construction at $453 billion. Percentage growth and dollar growth diverge Growth measured in percent and growth measured in dollars point to different segments. Residential added approximately $445 billion in outstanding balances since the first quarter of 2019 and core CRE added approximately $467 billion, each exceeding the roughly $229 billion added by multifamily, despite multifamily posting the largest percentage gain. The distinction matters for gauging where the largest absolute exposures have accumulated on bank balance sheets, as opposed to where growth has been proportionally most rapid. Construction and development: a distinct pattern Among the four categories, construction and development shows the most pronounced change in direction. Indexed to 2019, construction balances rose to a peak near 142 in 2024 before declining into early 2026, ending at 128. This rise-and-pa
bullets: U.S. bank multifamily loan balances rose 53% since Q1 2019; reached $665B by Q1 2026.
Multifamily grew fastest in % terms; residential ($3.1T) remains largest category.
Construction/development lending grew only 28%; cap-ex financing lagging multifamily debt growth.
stories.summary: Multifamily loan balances at U.S. banks surged 53% between Q1 2019 and Q1 2026, growing from baseline to $665 billion—the fastest percentage growth among all real estate categories. By Q1 2026, multifamily balances indexed to 153 (vs. 2019 baseline of 100), while construction/development lending only reached index 128. Residential mortgages remain the largest segment at $3.1 trillion.
stories.impact: Heavy loan growth signals lender confidence in multifamily stabilization and acquisition/hold strategies, but construction/development lending (index 128) is lagging multifamily portfolio growth (153). This suggests refinance/recapitalization activity is outpacing new construction financing—lenders prefer stabilized assets over development risk. Developers should expect tighter new-construction loan appetite and higher development loan pricing vs. acquisition financing; refinance windows for stabilized projects remain open.

stories.title: More Luxury Retailers Partner With Everyday Names to Lure Gen Z Shoppers
stories.excerpt: On May 16, watch brands Swatch and Audemars Piguet (AP) launched a collaborative line of eight pocket watches called Royal Pop, which, in addition to featuring designs that popped with vibrant colors, included a window into the inner workings of the watch. The intent of the Royal Pop watches, which retail for around $400 each, was to “invite a broader audience, including the younger generations, to experience mechanical watchmaking differently,” according to the AP website. As people throughout the world quickly learned, the collaboration worked almost too well. On the day of the new line’s unveiling, Swatch’s stores around the world were overrun. According to the Associated Press, police in Paris had to deploy tear gas to constrain the crowds, there were fistfights in Italy, and fans lined up throughout the night from New York to Singapore. As of two days after the product launch, Royal Pop’s social media feeds had received 11 billion views. While retail collaborations of this sort are hardly new, their frequency and popularity have been steadily rising in recent years as luxury brands like AP see creative downmarket collaborations as an effective way to introduce themselves to, and hopefully engender longtime brand loyalty from, younger consumers. Part of the reason why more brands decided to take on collaborations is that with so much information at their disposal, it’s easier to precisely Target consumers more inclined to appreciate both brands. “You have all of this access to information now, and you have all this social media online,” said Andrew Goldberg, a vice chairman at CBRE. “So you can go to Instagram, and you can see exactly who’s trending and who’s following who.” But Goldberg also sees some of these collaborations as necessary expansion. “So many of these brands have gotten big, and they’ve gotten to their core customer already,” said Goldberg. “They’re trying to think of how they can continue to develop and grow. All of these companies need to grow.
bullets: Luxury brands (Swatch, Audemars Piguet) collaborate on limited products; massive foot traffic.
Gen Z demand for experiential retail; collaborative launches drive store visits.
Retail demand signal; minimal multifamily relevance unless mixed-use retail has co-tenancy upside.
stories.summary: High-end watch brands Swatch and Audemars Piguet launched a limited collaborative line on May 16, driving extraordinary foot traffic and generating 11 billion social media views within two days. Retail collaborations are increasing in frequency, particularly targeting Gen Z through scarcity and experiential shopping. Crowds overwhelmed stores globally, with police intervention required in Paris.
stories.impact: Tangential to multifamily unless specific to mixed-use projects with retail components. Signal: younger renters (Gen Z) are driving experiential, walkable retail clusters, which supports higher rents in urban neighborhoods with dense retail/dining/bar density. Developers of urban infill multifamily should prioritize ground-floor retail/F&B activations and mixed-use positioning; luxury and collab-focused retail tenants can command premium rent, improving project feasibility. Relevant mainly for urban markets (NYC, LA, DC, Miami).

stories.title: The Plan: At the Windsor in Brooklyn’s Windsor Terrace, Assume Nothing
stories.excerpt: Windsor Terrace is a modest little neighborhood in southwest Brooklyn, perfectly situated a stone’s throw from Prospect Park, and an ideal location for a condominium development designed to entice families and individuals looking for unassuming luxury. Enter the Windsor at 23 Ocean Parkway, a red-brick building that is set back from the curb along a tranquil, tree-lined street, something Angely Rangel, sales director at Aguayo & Associates, the firm leading sales at the building, said is a huge draw for buyers. “A lot of the interest that we’re getting for the project, and the buyers that we’re getting, are actually local to the neighborhood,” Rangel said. “They want to stay in the area because they say that it’s a very peaceful area. But it’s almost like their little secret because where they actually live is very quiet, but it is just a couple of blocks to one stop away from all these amazing restaurants, cocktail bars and shopping.” The building, developed by Local Development Group, offers buyers 98 condominiums from studios to three-bedrooms ranging in price from $525,000 to $1.67 million, as well as 33 rental units designated as affordable housing under the state’s old 421-a tax abatement program. When developing the Windsor, which launched sales in June, Local Development Group wanted to ensure the new building didn’t stand out within the classic neighborhood, but rather fit in with the area’s aesthetic. “We wanted to keep the character of the neighborhood the way it is today,” Abraham Leifer, CEO of Local Development Group, said. “If you go down to that particular neighborhood, you’re not going to see a lot of new developments there, or fancy, glitzy buildings. We tried to conform with the neighborhood’s look.” Leifer said the last thing the firm wanted to do was put up a huge, obnoxious-looking building that didn’t fit in with the classic character of the area. “It shouldn’t feel like we are invaders to this neighborhood,” he said. “We’re here to help o
bullets: Windsor at 23 Ocean Parkway: 98-unit condo development in Brooklyn's Windsor Terrace.
Developer (Local Development Group) prioritizes local/neighborhood buyers seeking quiet, tree-lined appeal.
Condo sales (not multifamily rental); signal of brownstone/neighborhood gentrification vs. apartment trend.
stories.summary: The Windsor, a 98-unit condo building at 23 Ocean Parkway in Brooklyn's Windsor Terrace, is marketing to local, neighborhood-focused buyers drawn to the area's quiet, tree-lined character. Sales director notes strong local buyer interest and retention. Property features 18,000 sq ft of amenity space and a landscaped rooftop.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily rental development. This is condo/for-sale product in a historically renters'-market borough. Signal: Brooklyn's outer neighborhoods (Windsor Terrace) are stabilizing and attracting affluent local buyers seeking quality-of-life proximity, which suggests rental demand in adjacent blocks remains healthy. For rental developers in similar outer-Brooklyn micromarkets, this validates renter demand and supports higher rents; for condo developers, it confirms buyer appetite in quieter Brooklyn neighborhoods.

stories.title: United Talent Agency Will Relocate NYC Offices to Empire State Building
stories.excerpt: United Talent Agency (UTA) and Empire State Realty Trust, Inc. announced that the agency will move its New York offices to the Empire State Building next summer. The new office will feature 100,948 square feet across four floors and will house the agency’s filmed entertainment, creators, news, publishing, theatre, music, speakers, sports, brand advisory and executive search businesses. “We are pleased to welcome United Talent Agency to the ‘World’s Most Famous Building’ and to support the firm’s continued growth,” said Ryan Kass, EVP, co-head of real estate and chief revenue officer at ESRT. “The Empire State Building fosters collaboration, attracts top talent, enhances the employee experience, and is a premier destination for the world’s most innovative and influential businesses.” Mitchell Konsker, Benjamin Bass and Harrison Potter of JLL represented UTA in the lease negotiations. Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini, and Kerry Lavelle of ESRT and Scott Klau, Erik Harris, Neil Rubin, Brent Ozarowski, Zachary Weil and Cole Gendels of Newmark represented the property owner. The post United Talent Agency Will Relocate NYC Offices to Empire State Building appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: United Talent Agency leases 100,948 sq ft (4 floors) at Empire State Building; relocates summer 2027.
Large media/talent employer relocating within NYC; signals continued office occupancy for premier buildings.
Positive for Empire State Realty Trust; mixed signal for overall Manhattan office—trophy assets hold, commodity space remains vacant.
stories.summary: United Talent Agency committed to a large 100,948 sq ft lease across four floors at the Empire State Building, relocating its NYC operations from unspecified prior location. The move occurs summer 2027 and will house filmed entertainment, creators, news, publishing, theater, music, sports, and executive search divisions. ESRT and UTA highlighted collaboration and talent attraction as deal drivers.
stories.impact: NYC multifamily: UTA's retention in Midtown (rather than decentralization to hybrid/satellite offices) signals continued white-collar job clustering in Manhattan core. Media/talent sector jobs remain NYC-anchored, supporting rental demand for young professionals in Midtown South, Hell's Kitchen, and Upper West Side. Reinforces Manhattan rental fundamentals for luxury/amenity-rich multifamily near transit/Midtown job centers. Empire State Building occupancy signals trophy office assets outcompete Class B/C space, limiting overall Midtown vacancy pressure and stabilizing office-to-resi conversion pipeline (fewer quality conversions available).

stories.title: Original Developer Trades Castro Valley Multifamily Duo
stories.excerpt: Berkadia arranged the sale and acquisition financing of two value-add, neighboring Castro Valley multifamily communities with a total of 135 units. They included the Cedars, an 83-unit property that sold for $21.45 million, and Creekside Terrace, a 52-unit property that sold for $14.1 million. Senior managing director Jason Parr, managing director Scott MacDonald and director John Hansen of Berkadia San Francisco arranged the sale on behalf of Felson Companies, the original developer and longtime owner of both communities. Managing director Clay Akiwenzie and director Hank Workman of Berkadia San Francisco arranged a seven-year fixed-rate Freddie Mac loan with full-term interest-only on behalf of San Francisco-based Prime Residential. “Properties held by a single owner for over 50 years are incredibly rare in the Bay Area, and the value-add potential here drew significant investor demand,” said MacDonald. “Our investment sales team partnered closely with our mortgage banking colleagues to navigate a complex environment and deliver an exceptional outcome for our client.” The conversations behind tomorrow’s apartment deals happen at Connect Apartments on September 22 in LA. Connect with the capital sources, owners, developers, and brokers driving multifamily investment. Learn more and reserve your seat at www.connectapts2026.com. The post Original Developer Trades Castro Valley Multifamily Duo appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Felson Companies (original developer, 50+ yr hold) trades Castro Valley duo: 135 units total for $35.55M.
Cedars (83 units, $21.45M); Creekside Terrace (52 units, $14.1M); both value-add plays.
Freddie Mac financing (7-yr fixed, full IO); buyer Prime Residential; Bay Area stabilized-asset liquidity solid.
stories.summary: Castro Valley multifamily duo—Cedars (83 units, $21.45M) and Creekside Terrace (52 units, $14.1M)—sold by original developer Felson Companies after 50+ year hold. Combined sale $35.55M for 135 units (~$263K/unit). Buyer Prime Residential secured Freddie Mac fixed-rate financing (7-year, IO). Value-add investor demand was strong per Berkadia.
stories.impact: Bay Area (East Bay) multifamily market: $263K per unit on 135-unit portfolio suggests mid-market stabilized asset pricing remains firm. Freddie Mac full-interest-only financing availability indicates lender confidence in Bay Area rental fundamentals despite broader cap rate compression. Value-add investor appetite high for legacy, undermanaged properties—signals shortage of quality, stabilized product in East Bay suburbs. Developers should consider Bay Area infill/value-add acquisitions; new ground-up development in Castro Valley faces cost-to-value challenges vs. acquisition/reposition strategy.

stories.title: Resorts World NYC Adds 1,400 Slot Machines as Part of $5.5B Expansion
stories.excerpt: Resorts World New York City on Tuesday opened the first floor of its commercial casino, adding 1,400 slot machines less than three months after the new facility became the city’s first full-fledged casino. The move, which brings the number of slot machines onsite to 3,900, is expected to significantly increase gross gaming revenue and commensurate taxes to New York State, according to Resorts World. “Since the moment we opened, our slot machines have been incredibly popular with the public, generating more than $230 million in gross gaming revenue in just 11 weeks,” said Robert DeSalvio, president of Genting Americas East. “With these 1,400 new games, we will be able to accommodate more patrons and generate even more in taxes for the state.” The gaming operation represents the first stage of a planned $5.5-billion buildout at the site. By 2030, RWNYC intends to operate a 350,000-square-foot gaming floor with 6,000 slot machines and 800 live table games, a 7,000-seat entertainment venue, 2,000 hotel rooms and more than 10 acres of public green space. The post Resorts World NYC Adds 1,400 Slot Machines as Part of $5.5B Expansion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Resorts World NYC opened casino floor 1 with 1,400 slots; 3,900 total onsite; $5.5B phased buildout planned.
Generated $230M gross gaming revenue in 11 weeks; expanding to 2,000 hotel rooms and 350K sq ft gaming floor by 2030.
Major employment cluster, visitor draw, and tax revenue influx for NYC; indirect residential demand signal.
stories.summary: Resorts World NYC, the city's first full-fledged casino, opened floor 1 with 1,400 new slot machines (total 3,900), achieving $230M in gross gaming revenue in 11 weeks. The operator plans a $5.5B phased expansion through 2030, including 2,000 hotel rooms, 7,000-seat entertainment venue, 800 table games, and 10 acres of green space.
stories.impact: Queens (Aqueduct location) multifamily: Massive employment influx from casino operations, hotel, entertainment, and construction jobs over next 5 years drives renter demand in Queens (Astoria, Long Island City, Woodside nearby corridors). Casino guest workers and service staff create sustained local rental demand. Secondary effect: RWNYC amenity/entertainment cluster attracts young renters seeking urban nightlife within Queens. Developers should prioritize multifamily sites 1-3 miles from RWNYC; 2–3 bedroom apartments and studio/1BR units for hospitality workers will see strong absorption. Land values in adjacent Queens micromarkets may appreciate; land banking now provides future development upside.

stories.title: Calare Acquires Industrial in Boston’s Hyde Park Neighborhood
stories.excerpt: Calare Properties has acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, a 42,000-square-foot industrial warehouse/flex condominium in Boston’s Hyde Park section. Terms were not disclosed. The urban property sits within a 22-acre, 10-building industrial and mixed-use site with a manufacturing history dating back to the early 1900s. 128 CRE brokered the sale. The building is nearly 50% leased to a long-term tenant. The available 24,000-square-foot space provides tenants with an opportunity to secure a long-term lease in a market where quality industrial space is scarce, according to Calare. The new ownership is executing a capital improvement plan. “Quality industrial buildings of this scale are exceptionally rare in Boston, and that scarcity is exactly what drives our strategy,” said Tim Collis, SVP at Calare. “Being part of an established, well-run campus gives us both immediate stability and long-term upside as we execute our planned capital program and long-term leasing strategy.” The post Calare Acquires Industrial in Boston’s Hyde Park Neighborhood appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Calare acquires One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, 42K sq ft industrial in Boston's Hyde Park, 50% leased.
Urban industrial scarcity in Boston; 22-acre campus offers stability + long-term value-add leasing opportunity.
Industrial asset class (not multifamily); tangential signal on Boston development constraints and land scarcity.
stories.summary: Calare Properties acquired One Westinghouse Plaza Building K, a 42,000 sq ft warehouse/flex condominium in Hyde Park, Boston's former manufacturing district. Building is 50% leased; available 24,000 sq ft represents scarcity premium. Property sits on 22-acre, 10-building campus with 1900s industrial roots. Calare executing capital plan to fill remaining space.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily relevance. Signal for Boston development landscape: urban industrial scarcity (per Calare quote) indicates tight land/building supply city-wide. Regulatory/zoning constraints limiting new industrial supply can also affect multifamily entitlement timelines and construction logistics. Hyde Park's industrial-to-mixed-use potential (if future conversion emerges) could support multifamily infill—but this deal is stabilization/value-add industrial, not residential conversion.

stories.title: Fresno Apartments Fetch $44M in First-Ever Sale
stories.excerpt: The Mogharebi Group (TMG) represented JBT Property Management in the sale of Maroa Park Apartments, a 248-unit multifamily community located at 475-585 W. Sierra Ave. in Fresno, to a private buyer for $44 million. TMG’s team of EVP Otto Ozen, SVP Brian Nakamura and Svp Nazli Santana represented the seller. “As a first-time-on-the-market property since its construction in 1986, the asset offered a rare opportunity to acquire a well-maintained, large-scale property directly from original ownership,” said Nakamura. “With its substantial unit count in a supply‑constrained market, the property immediately enhances the buyer’s regional presence—supporting stronger operational alignment, improved management efficiency, and meaningful performance gains, all without requiring significant capital investment.” Maroa Park Apartments benefits from immediate income upside through operational efficiencies while benefiting from strong in-place market fundamentals, relative affordability, and a strategic Central Valley location that continues to support long-term population and employment growth, according to TMG The post Fresno Apartments Fetch $44M in First-Ever Sale appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Maroa Park Apartments (248 units, Fresno) sells for $44M; first-ever sale since 1986 construction; $177K/unit.
Supply-constrained Fresno market; original owner liquidation signals stabilized income, long-term hold exit.
Central Valley affordability and population growth support long-term rental fundamentals.
stories.summary: JBT Property Management sold Maroa Park Apartments, a 248-unit community in Fresno at 475-585 W. Sierra Ave., to a private buyer for $44 million ($177K per unit). Original developer held asset since 1986; first-ever market sale. TMG emphasized supply scarcity, operational efficiencies, and regional portfolio consolidation upside.
stories.impact: Fresno (Central Valley) multifamily: $177K/unit pricing on 248-unit stabilized asset reflects Fresno's affordable-market positioning and supply scarcity. First-sale liquidation by original owner suggests long-hold stability and predictable income—positive signal for investor confidence in Fresno fundamentals. Central Valley population/employment growth (per TMG commentary) supports new-build development feasibility. Developers should evaluate Fresno infill and greenfield multifamily; sub-$50K/unit development costs and rent growth (vs. Bay Area) offer stronger IRR profiles. Regional population migration from expensive CA metros (Bay, LA) to Central Valley drives demand—development window open for next 3–5 years.

stories.title: South Florida’s top deals: Telecom exec pays $19M for new Miami mansion
stories.excerpt: 🏆 Residential: The priciest home sale to hit records in South Florida was in Miami’s Ponce-Davis neighborhood. An LLC managed by Christophe Scherman parted with the roughly 10,000-square-foot mansion at 5545 Southwest 81st Terrace for $18.9 million. An entity led by telecommunications executive Meinrad Spenger bought the house. Built this year, the home sits on a nearly 1-acre lot and has seven bedrooms and seven and a half bathrooms. It also has a separate guest house. David Siddons with Douglas Elliman represented the seller, and Wesley Ulloa and Christopher Borchetta with Luxe Properties brought the buyer. The home’s most recent […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Telecom exec (Meinrad Spenger) buys $18.9M Miami mansion; Jupiter waterfront $10.5M; high-net-worth liquidity flowing to South Florida.
Single-family luxury sales in Ponce-Davis, Jupiter; real estate activity signals affluent in-migration.
Indirect rental demand: wealthy buyer cohort often brings family office, staff, service workers requiring local housing.
stories.summary: Luxury residential transactions in South Florida: telecom executive Meinrad Spenger purchased a newly built 10,000+ sq ft mansion in Miami's Ponce-Davis for $18.9M. In Jupiter, a waterfront home sold for $10.5M (2022 purchase price $8M). Both sales reflect strong high-net-worth buyer activity in Miami-Dade and Palm Beach counties.
stories.impact: South Florida (Miami-Dade, Palm Beach) multifamily: High-net-worth migration (particularly C-suite telecom/tech executives) signals executive job growth and in-migration to South Florida. Secondary effect: affluent buyers bring staff, advisors, and service workers requiring local apartments. Multifamily developers should focus on supply-constrained submarkets (Wynwood, Brickell, Design District, Coconut Grove) where young professionals and service workers seek walkable, urban rental. Luxury sales velocity also signals land value appreciation—land banking near wealthy residential clusters (Coconut Grove, Coral Gables) positions developers for future multifamily upside as neighborhoods densify.

stories.title: Elysian, Capstone go 100% affordable for Warner Center apartment project
stories.excerpt: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities are planning to build a new affordable housing development in Woodland Hills.  Los Angeles-based Elysian Housing and New York-based Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street, L.A. Business First reported. A one-story, 35,000-square-foot commercial building at the site would be demolished to make way for more than 300 units of income-restricted housing. Terms of the transaction, including price, were not disclosed.  Elysian and Capstone plan to erect 316 affordable units across seven stories on the 2.7 acre property. The proposed project is tied to the largest new construction permit of the first week of this month, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Elysian Housing + Capstone Equities acquire 21155 Califa St (Woodland Hills); 316 affordable units, 7-story build.
The Green at Warner Center; expedited approval under LA Executive Directive 1; Kroenke's $52-acre mixed-use development nearby.
Affordable housing supply pipeline in Warner Center; Woodland Hills becoming affordable housing hub amid major institutional investment.
stories.summary: Elysian Housing and Capstone Equities purchased 21155 Califa Street in Woodland Hills, LA to build The Green at Warner Center, a 316-unit all-affordable development on 2.7 acres. Project qualifies for expedited approval under LA's Executive Directive 1. Separate Variel Avenue permit filed for same developers. Development follows Kroenke's major $52-acre mixed-use master plan (Rams HQ site) in Woodland Hills.
stories.impact: Los Angeles (Woodland Hills/San Fernando Valley) multifamily: Institutional capital (Elysian + Capstone) targeting all-affordable development in Woodland Hills signals shift in valley land positioning. Kroenke's mixed-use development ($52 acres + Rams HQ) anchors Woodland Hills as employment/mixed-use hub, driving workforce housing demand. Expedited approval and Executive Directive 1 streamline 100% affordable projects, reducing entitlement risk for developers. Market implication: Warner Center affordability and density will increase, lowering rents in valley submarkets. For-profit developers should pivot to market-rate projects in adjacent areas (Canoga Park, Winnetka) or accept margin compression; affordability restrictions will cap rents in Woodland Hills proper.

stories.title: Austin suburb selects Griffin Swinerton to revamp downtown
stories.excerpt: One of the nation’s fastest growing suburbs is enlisting a new developer to bring its downtown space up to code as it welcomes in a steady influx of new residents.  Liberty Hill’s city officials selected San Francisco-based Griffin Swinerton, a joint venture of Griffin Structures Incorporated and builder Swinerton Incorporated to head up the downtown facelift. The joint venture will officially function as though they were city staff, according to the Austin Business Journal.  The responsibility extends beyond listening to what the community wants and then delivering. Griffin Swinerton will be tasked with actively soliciting community thoughts as well as […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Liberty Hill (Austin suburb, fastest-growing US suburb) hires Griffin Swinerton for downtown revamp; 2028 target start.
Masterplan adopted 2024; focus on walkability, historic preservation, community gathering space, parking, industry districts.
Population boom (3x since 2020 census) outpacing downtown infrastructure; placemaking investment precedes residential density wave.
stories.summary: Liberty Hill, one of the nation's fastest-growing suburbs, selected Griffin Swinerton (SF-based JV) to lead downtown revitalization, targeting 2028 start. Project includes infrastructure evaluation, historic preservation, parking upgrades, and industry-district planning. City adopted masterplan in 2024 and tripled in size since 2020 census.
stories.impact: Austin metro (Liberty Hill, TX) multifamily: Population boom (3x since 2020) is outpacing downtown infrastructure; Griffin Swinerton's assignment signals city-led infill/placemaking investment creating walkable, mixed-use downtown. Precedent: Griffin Swinerton completed similar work in Pflugerville (29-acre Downtown East project). Implication: downtown revamp will attract anchor tenants, entertainment, retail, and dining—supporting new multifamily development adjacent to downtown core. Developers should acquire land near Liberty Hill downtown for 2028–2030 delivery timing (pre-revamp completion). Population pressure + placemaking investment = strong rental demand signal for market-rate and workforce housing. Austin metro remains constrained for supply; Liberty Hill density will relieve pressure and attract regional development capital.

stories.title: CMK grabs key permit to launch 28-story South Loop high-rise
stories.excerpt: A planned South Loop high-rise is all but ready for shovels to break ground. The CMK Companies’ planned mixed-use development at 910 South Wells just snagged its piles permit, and work on the foundation can commence.  CMK already received site plan approval last November, according to Urbanize Chicago. Gensler is heading up the design for the 28-story building, which will feature 3,300 square feet of space for retail and 350 residential units. Plans also include ample parking space, an outdoor amenity space for residents and a riverwalk view. The building will be connected to the riverwalk using a pedestrian path. […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: CMK obtains piles permit for 910 South Wells, Chicago South Loop; 28-story, 350-unit mixed-use tower.
Riverline Parcel F Building B part of $2B truncated master plan (CMK 8 acres; Lendlease completed 2 towers).
CMK secured $83M Wintrust construction loan for Phase 1 (1010 South Wells, 388 units); foundation work active.
stories.summary: CMK Companies' 910 South Wells (Riverline Parcel F Building B) obtained piles permit; 28-story, 350-unit residential with 3,300 sq ft retail and riverwalk connectivity. Part of $2B Riverline master plan (8 acres; split 2018 from original 15-acre partnership with Lendlease). CMK also secured $83M Wintrust construction loan for 1010 South Wells Phase 1 (388 units); both projects use Brandt's Build.
stories.impact: Chicago South Loop multifamily: CMK's active construction (piles permit + $83M loan) signals strong lender appetite for high-density urban multifamily in 24-hr neighborhood. Riverline master plan buildout (700+ units across phases) will increase South Loop supply, moderating rents short-term but anchoring long-term neighborhood credibility. Riverwalk connectivity and mixed-use positioning attract renters seeking urban walkability—supports premium rent capture. Developers should monitor South Loop supply pipeline and focus on differentiation (luxury finishes, tech amenities, unique location/access) vs. commodity product. Early lease-up windows (2027–2028) will be competitive; later phases (2029+) may see stabilized rents as market absorbs supply.

stories.title: Therme Dallas pivots, adds skybridge to $800M wellness resort buildout plans
stories.excerpt: Therme Dallas is looking for city approval to add a pedestrian skybridge and new non-residential zones to their $800 million resort development.  The Austrian wellness company is looking to break ground on the 24-acre, 500,000-square-foot resort in 2027. Plans for the massive resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard include swim-up bars, thermal baths, saunas and restaurants, all within a beach-like atmosphere with a retractable glass roof. The skybridge will connect two of the planned buildings, according to the Dallas Business Journal, but which two is unclear.  Therme raised eyebrows around a year ago when it purchased the land for the […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Therme Dallas ($800M wellness resort) targets 2027 groundbreaking; adds skybridge, 24 acres, 500K sq ft.
NY Times exposed Therme misrepresentation of development experience (claimed 6 facilities; only 1 built in Bucharest).
Credibility and delivery risk; may trigger capital market scrutiny and regulatory delays; deal contingent on Dallas city approvals.
stories.summary: Therme Dallas, an $800M Austrian wellness resort on 1500 South Riverfront Boulevard, is seeking approval for skybridge and new non-residential zones, targeting 2027 groundbreaking. Plans include thermal baths, saunas, restaurants, retractable glass roof, beach atmosphere on 24 acres. However, NY Times exposed Therme misrepresenting its development track record (claimed six facilities; only one completed in Bucharest).
stories.impact: Dallas (Trinity River waterfront) multifamily: Therme credibility issues may delay/derail project, limiting indirect demand drivers (employment, visitor lodging, mixed-use amenity clustering). If project proceeds, it will anchor Trinity River as major attraction, attracting renter demographics seeking walkable urban resort/recreation lifestyle. Secondary effect: concurrent Water Commons (urban wetlands) and Harold Simmons Park development create amenity-rich microclimate supporting premium apartment rents. For multifamily developers: do NOT assume Therme delivery; plan independently for Trinity River demand. If Therme delays >2 years, development window for adjacent multifamily shifts 3–5 years right. Conversely, if Therme delivers, land values within 0.5 mi appreciate sharply; land acquisitions now offer optionality for future phases.

stories.title: Tannos secures $28M in construction financing for Houston area mixed-use project
stories.excerpt: Tannos Development Groups’ Friendswood mixed-use project is one step closer to coming to fruition.  The Friendswood-based developer secured $28 million in mid-construction financing for The Albritton, a mixed-use development being built at 408 South Friendswood Drive, according to a release from Miami-based Concord Summit Capital, which arranged the deal.  Concord Summit’s Daniel J. Eidson and Ben Applebaum sourced the financing. The debt, which has a 70 percent loan-to-value ratio, is supposed to carry the project “through the remainder of construction,” Eidson said in the release. S3 Capital provided the financing, Commercial Observer reported.  The Albritton will feature 111 multifamily units, […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Tannos Development secures $28M mid-construction financing (70% LTV) for The Albritton (111 units, Friendswood, TX).
111 multifamily units + 11K sq ft retail + 9K sq ft office; completion 2028; S3 Capital provided debt.
Friendswood (25 mi SE Houston, 42K residents) emerging as mixed-use development hub; Tannos also building Magnolia Town Center ($1B) and Friendswood City Center ($750M).
stories.summary: Tannos Development secured $28M construction financing from S3 Capital for The Albritton, a mixed-use project in Friendswood, TX featuring 111 multifamily units, 11,000 sq ft ground-floor retail, and 9,000 sq ft office. 70% LTV loan carries project through completion in 2028. Friendswood, 25 miles SE of Houston, is Tannos's hub; firm also developing Magnolia Town Center ($1B, 200 acres) and Friendswood City Center ($750M, 106 acres).
stories.impact: Houston metro (Friendswood/Magnolia, TX) multifamily: Tannos's $28M mid-stage construction financing (70% LTV, S3 Capital) signals lender confidence in Houston suburban mixed-use fundamentals. Tannos's triple-project portfolio ($1B Magnolia + $750M Friendswood City Center + $28M Albritton) demonstrates developer scaling and strong capital access—signals robust Houston investor appetite. Friendswood location (equidistant Houston/Galveston, 42K residents) positions projects for affordable workforce + leisure visitor hybrid demand. Supply wave (500+ units across Tannos projects by 2028+) will moderate Friendswood/Magnolia rents; entry-level rents (1BR, studios) will face pressure. Developers should focus on premium product (2BR+, luxury finishes) or workforce housing (tax credits, affordability) to differentiate. Development window remains open; land banking in adjacent Pearland, Texas City extends optionality as Friendswood absorbs.
